title: Battenberg cake
subtitle: British checkerboard almond sponge wrapped in marzipan
excerpt: A two-colour sponge baked in a divided tin, cut into four strips, stuck together in alternating colours with warmed apricot jam, and wrapped in rolled marzipan. The cross-section shows a neat pink and yellow checkerboard. Almond flour in the batter gives the sponge a slightly denser, moister crumb than a plain Victoria. The marzipan wrapper is pressed to seal underneath and the top surface is lightly scored.
sourceNotes: Battenberg cake is a British teatime cake first documented in the late nineteenth century. It is widely attributed to the 1884 marriage of Prince Louis of Battenberg to Princess Victoria of Hesse, though the exact origin of the name is uncertain. Mrs Isabella Beeton, Book of Household Management (revised 1906 edition, Project Gutenberg #10136), includes a recipe consistent with the checkerboard assembly and marzipan wrapping that define the modern cake. The pink-and-yellow colour combination and the four-square structure are consistent across British baking literature from the Edwardian period onwards.

content.text: The signature look depends on precise

: two slabs of sponge trimmed to identical size, cut into strips, and stacked in a 2-by-2 grid with alternating colours. The apricot jam must be warm enough to spread and stick but not so hot it soaks into the sponge. The marzipan wrapper is rolled to a thin sheet just before assembly.

Heat the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan). Line a 20cm square tin with baking paper. Create a central divider: fold a doubled strip of foil and press it down the middle of the tin, or use a purpose-made Battenberg tin.

Beat the butter and sugar until pale and fluffy. Beat in the eggs one at a time, adding a tablespoon of flour with each one to prevent curdling. Fold in the remaining flour, almond flour, vanilla, and salt. Add milk if the batter is stiff. Divide evenly into two bowls. Stir the pink food colouring into one half until uniformly coloured. Spoon each batter into one side of the divided tin. Bake for 28 to 32 minutes until a skewer comes out clean. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then on a rack.

Trim both slabs to equal rectangles of the same height and length. Cut each into two equal strips lengthways (four strips total, two pink, two yellow). Brush the long sides of each strip with warm apricot jam and press them together in a 2-by-2 checkerboard: pink next to yellow in the first row, yellow next to pink in the second. Brush the outside of the assembled log with jam.

Dust the work surface with icing sugar. Roll the marzipan into a rectangle large enough to wrap the log (about 25 x 40 cm). Place the log at one short edge and roll the marzipan firmly around it. Press to seal the seam underneath. Trim the ends neatly. Score the top of the marzipan lightly in a diamond pattern if you like. Store uncovered at room temperature and eat within 3 days.
